class_schedule.topic: Introduction to I/O Psychology: Science and Practice
learning_objectives: Define I/O psychology and distinguish industrial from organizational sides
Describe the scientist-practitioner model in workplace settings
Identify key ethical issues in I/O practice
readings: Spector, Industrial and Organizational Psychology, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: I/O psychology applies psychological science to the workplace, addressing both the efficiency of organizations and the well-being of workers.
key_concepts: Industrial side: selection, training, performance appraisal, job analysis
Organizational side: motivation, leadership, culture, job satisfaction
History: Munsterberg, Hawthorne studies, Army Alpha/Beta tests
Scientist-practitioner model in business contexts
SIOP guidelines and ethical practice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study: analyze a real company's approach to selection and identify where I/O psychology principles could improve outcomes

review.value: Workers' productivity improved simply from the attention of being studied; highlighted the importance of social and psychological factors in the workplace
Industrial side focuses on fitting people to jobs (selection, training); organizational side focuses on understanding people at work (motivation, leadership)

learning_objectives: Describe methods of job analysis and their applications
Explain the validity of different selection methods (meta-analytic evidence)
Discuss legal and ethical considerations in employee selection

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective selection begins with understanding what the job requires. Job analysis provides the foundation for all HR functions.
key_concepts: Job analysis methods: observation, interviews, questionnaires (PAQ, O*NET)
KSAOs: knowledge, skills, abilities, and other characteristics
Selection methods ranked by validity: GMA tests, structured interviews, work samples, assessment centers
Criterion-related validity: predictive and concurrent designs
Adverse impact, four-fifths rule, and legal compliance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a brief job analysis for a familiar role (university RA) and develop selection criteria

review.value: Highest validity when questions are job-related, standardized, and scored with behavioral anchors; much more valid than unstructured interviews
GMA tests are the single best predictor of job performance across virtually all occupations (meta-analytic r ≈ .51)

learning_objectives: Compare content and process theories of work motivation
Describe the Job Characteristics Model and its predictions
Explain the relationship between job satisfaction and organizational outcomes

lecture_notes.introduction: Motivated, satisfied employees are the foundation of effective organizations. Understanding what drives people at work allows organizations to design jobs and environments that bring out the best in people.
key_concepts: Maslow's hierarchy and Herzberg's two-factor theory (historical context)
Expectancy theory (Vroom): expectancy x instrumentality x valence
Goal-setting theory (Locke & Latham): specific difficult goals enhance performance
Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham): skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, feedback
Job satisfaction measurement and its link to turnover, absenteeism, and performance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesign a monotonous job using the Job Characteristics Model to enhance motivation

review.value: Specific, difficult goals produce higher performance than 'do your best' goals; feedback and commitment are key moderators
Five core job characteristics lead to three psychological states (meaningfulness, responsibility, knowledge of results) that produce high motivation, satisfaction, and performance

learning_objectives: Compare transformational, transactional, and servant leadership models
Explain Leader-Member Exchange theory and its implications
Describe organizational citizenship behavior and counterproductive work behavior

lecture_notes.introduction: Leadership is one of the most studied topics in I/O psychology. Modern research has moved beyond traits to examine leader behaviors, relationships, and contextual factors.
key_concepts: Transformational leadership: idealized influence, inspirational motivation, intellectual stimulation, individualized consideration
Transactional leadership: contingent reward, management by exception
Leader-Member Exchange (LMX): quality of leader-follower relationships varies
Organizational citizenship behavior (OCB): helping, conscientiousness, sportsmanship
Counterproductive work behavior (CWB): theft, aggression, withdrawal
lecture_notes.demonstration: Leadership style assessment: students complete the MLQ and discuss implications for different organizational contexts

review.value: Leaders inspire followers to transcend self-interest for the good of the organization; consistently associated with higher satisfaction, commitment, and performance
Voluntary behaviors not part of the formal job description but essential for organizational functioning; predicted by job satisfaction and perceived fairness

learning_objectives: Distinguish distributive, procedural, and interactional justice
Explain the impact of perceived injustice on employee behavior
Describe occupational stress models and burnout prevention

lecture_notes.introduction: Fairness perceptions profoundly affect workplace attitudes and behavior. Organizations that are perceived as just retain better employees and experience less conflict.
key_concepts: Distributive justice: fairness of outcomes (equity theory)
Procedural justice: fairness of decision-making processes
Interactional justice: fairness of interpersonal treatment
Demands-resources model of occupational stress (Bakker & Demerouti)
Burnout dimensions: emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, reduced accomplishment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case analysis: identify justice violations in a corporate restructuring scenario and predict employee responses

review.value: Perceived fairness of processes used to make decisions; people accept unfavorable outcomes better when the process was fair (voice, consistency, accuracy)
Maslach: chronic occupational stress leading to emotional exhaustion, depersonalization/cynicism, and reduced personal accomplishment; predicted by high demands and low resources

class_schedule.topic: Training, Performance Management, and the Future of Work
learning_objectives: Describe the systematic training design process and transfer of training
Compare performance appraisal methods and rater biases
Discuss emerging I/O topics: remote work, AI in HR, gig economy

lecture_notes.introduction: Training and performance management are core functions of I/O psychology. Rapid changes in the nature of work demand continuous adaptation in how organizations develop and evaluate their people.
key_concepts: ADDIE model: analysis, design, development, implementation, evaluation
Transfer of training: near vs. far; factors promoting transfer
Performance appraisal methods: BARS, BOS, 360-degree feedback
Rater errors: halo, leniency, central tendency, recency
Future of work: remote/hybrid, algorithmic management, continuous feedback systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a training needs analysis for a common workplace skill gap scenario

review.value: Degree to which learned skills are applied on the job; influenced by training design, trainee characteristics, and work environment support
Performance input from supervisors, peers, subordinates, and self; provides comprehensive perspective but can be influenced by relationship quality

questions.question: A company uses an unstructured interview as its primary selection tool. Using meta-analytic evidence, explain why this is problematic and recommend an evidence-based alternative with its approximate validity coefficient.
questions.answer: Unstructured interviews have low predictive validity (r ≈ .20) because they are inconsistent across candidates, susceptible to interviewer biases (halo, similarity, first impression), and often assess irrelevant characteristics. Evidence-based alternative: structured interviews (r ≈ .51) use job-related questions derived from job analysis, standardized across all candidates, with behavioral anchoring scales for scoring. Combined with a GMA test (r ≈ .51), structured interviews provide incremental validity. Work sample tests (r ≈ .54) are also excellent alternatives for jobs where tasks can be simulated.

rubric.key: Identifies low validity of unstructured interviews with evidence
Recommends structured interview or work sample with validity
Explains why the alternative is superior

questions.question: Using the Job Characteristics Model, explain why a factory assembly line worker who tightens the same bolt 500 times per day is likely to be unmotivated, and propose three specific job redesign strategies.
questions.answer: The JCM predicts low motivation because the job scores low on all five core dimensions: low skill variety (one repetitive task), low task identity (small piece of total product), low task significance (no connection to end user), low autonomy (pace dictated by line), and low feedback (no information about quality). Three redesign strategies: (1) Job enlargement for skill variety: add quality inspection and minor repair tasks. (2) Natural work units for task identity: assign complete subassembly rather than single bolt. (3) Vertical loading for autonomy: give workers authority to stop the line for quality issues and participate in process improvement decisions.

rubric.key: Correctly applies all five JCM dimensions
Proposes three specific, distinct redesign strategies
Links strategies to specific JCM dimensions

questions.question: An employee receives a smaller bonus than a colleague despite believing they performed equally well. Using organizational justice theory, describe three types of justice perceptions that will influence the employee's reaction and predict their likely response if all three are violated.
questions.answer: Distributive justice: the employee perceives the outcome (bonus) as unfair relative to their inputs compared to the colleague. Procedural justice: if the process for determining bonuses was opaque, inconsistent, or lacked employee voice, procedural injustice is perceived. Interactional justice: if the manager delivered the news dismissively, without explanation or empathy, interactional injustice is added. When all three are violated, the employee is likely to: reduce effort (equity restoration), engage in counterproductive work behavior (withdrawal, complaining), search for alternative employment, or file a formal grievance. Multiple justice violations produce the strongest negative reactions.

rubric.key: Correctly describes all three justice types in context
Notes the compounding effect of multiple violations

questions.question: Describe two common rater errors in performance appraisal and explain one specific technique for reducing each.
questions.answer: Halo error: rating an employee high on all dimensions because they excel on one dimension (or low across all because of one weakness). Reduction: use Behaviorally Anchored Rating Scales (BARS) with specific behavioral examples for each dimension, forcing raters to evaluate each dimension independently with concrete anchors. Leniency error: rating all employees higher than their actual performance warrants, compressing ratings toward the top. Reduction: forced distribution systems or frame-of-reference training where raters are calibrated by evaluating the same set of benchmark performances, establishing shared standards.

rubric.key: Explains why each technique addresses the error

major_assignments.title: Organizational Analysis and Intervention Proposal
major_assignments.description: Select a real organization (or a detailed case study) experiencing a workplace problem (turnover, low engagement, poor leadership, unfair selection). Conduct an analysis using I/O psychology frameworks, diagnose the problem, and propose an evidence-based intervention.
objectives: Apply I/O psychology theories and research to a real organizational problem
Conduct a systematic organizational analysis using appropriate frameworks
Design an evidence-based intervention with implementation plan
Evaluate the expected impact using I/O psychology metrics
deliverables: 3,000-word organizational analysis and proposal in APA format
Detailed intervention plan with timeline and budget estimate

criteria: Thorough analysis using appropriate I/O frameworks
Identifies root causes not just symptoms
Uses data and evidence to support diagnosis
Considers both efficiency and employee well-being
Professional writing suitable for business audience
Thorough organizational analysis using I/O frameworks
Evidence-based intervention with detailed implementation plan
Rigorous evaluation plan with measurable indicators
Professional writing with strong research base

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of I/O psychology concepts and applications

case_analyses.description: Analysis of real-world organizational cases using I/O frameworks

major_assignments.description: Organizational analysis and evidence-based intervention proposal

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering selection, motivation, and leadership

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. Case analysis discussions require active participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. The organizational analysis will not be accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Case analyses may be discussed in groups. All written assignments must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citations. Company data used in analyses must be publicly available or properly disguised.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to O*NET database, SIOP resources, and university library databases.
